coredata.dc:title: High frequency electrochemical transmission spectroscopy application to bulk conducting analytes in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Johnston, Derek
Karp, Tanja
Pappas, Dimitri
Christopher, Gordon
coredata.dc:description: This work illustrates a novel technique, high-frequency transmission spectroscopy (HFTS), for the characterization of analyte in aqueous solution. The application of this technique to material characterization is similar traditional impedance spectroscopy (IS) but differs primarily in that HFTS is a measure of transmitted potential through the material (voltage out versus voltage in) rather than its impedance (current out versus voltage in). The material properties explored by HFTS are not limited to effects occurring at the electrode/analyte interface which dominate IS measurements. Additionally, electric network analyzers (ENA), used for the characterization of frequency response, provide spectrum measurement capability over a much higher frequency range than typical impedance instrumentation. In this work, the capability of HFTS is demonstrated using three test cases: electrolyte solutions, carbon nanoparticles in solution, and sulfate latex microparticles in aqueous suspension. The experimental setup consists of a 3D printed liquid test cell, custom design patch antennae, and an off-the shelf ENA with a frequency range of 300 kHz to 500 MHz. The magnitude response of these analytes at various concentrations demonstrated a sensitivity of the measurement on the order of 10 µM. Furthermore, the system shows the ability to distinguish between monovalent and divalent ions and detect changes in radii on the order of 10 pm to 1 µm. Techniques demonstrated in this work have a wide range of potential applications including environmental monitoring, detection of polluting substances (e.g. PFAS) in water, and disease/infection screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Scattering parameters
Electrolyte solution
Carbon Dots
Carbon nanoparticles
Plastic microparticles
Impedance spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The basic model of a 2-port electrical network with scattering parameters shown. S11 and S22 parameters represent the reflectance of the MOI and S21 and S12 represent its transmittance. In this work, the S21 parameter is used.

The lumped-element circuit model of the S21 measurement. Vs is the AC potential perturbation. The impedances Z0 are the input and output impedance of the electric network analyzer and the impedance Zm is the impedance of the material of interest. The potentials V1 and V2 represent the applied excitation potential and the received excitation potential, respectively. In contrast to impedance spectroscopy, which seeks to measure the impedance of the MOI itself, transmission spectroscopy is concerned with the ratio of the two excitation potentials.

The simplified impedance model for the experimental setup. The electric network analyzer (ENA) performs the scattering parameter measurement. Each port of the ENA has a source impedance Z0 which are standardized to 50Ω. Each antenna electrode has an impedance based upon its geometry and the dielectric constant of the substrate.

The mean S21 measurement of ultra-pure H_2 O in the 2mL test cell overlaid a plot of the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the measurement. A peak response of 0.86 dB is clearly visible at ∼60 MHz. This frequency also gives the peak SNR of 105.3dB.

Simulation results of an analogous circuit model which illustrates the effect of additional shunt capacitance at the input and output of the ports of a two-port network. A decrease in resonance frequency along with a decrease in frequency spacing between the peaks is observed with increase in shunt capacitance.

The S21 magnitude response of the test cell containing varying concentrations of NaCl in H_2 O. Dilutions from 15μM to 15mM show a clear inverse correlation with peak response. The response from 150mM shows the emergence of another peak at a lower frequency (14.5 MHz)

Comparisons of different electrolyte compounds in aqueous solution at concentrations of 150mM and 15mM. (a) NaCl versus KCl: The independent variable is the size of the cation. The Na^+ ion has an atomic radius of 190pm and the K^+ ion has a radius of 243pm. At 150mM, NaCl has a magnitude response of 0.46 (0.21 at 15mM) versus the KCl response of 0.42 (0.20 at 15mM). (b) NaCl versus NaNO_3: The independent variable is anion size with Cl^- having a radius of 175pm while the radius of NO_3 is 177pm. At 150mM, NaNO_3 has a magnitude response of 0.37 (0.21 at 15mM). (c) NaCl versus MgCl_2: The IV is cation charge with Na^(+1) and Mg^(+2). At 150mM, MgCl_2 has a magnitude response of 0.35 (0.22 at 15mM). (d) Mg(NO_3)_2 versus MgCl_2. These molecules are very similar with respect to cation size, anion size, and charge. At 150mM, Mg(NO_3)_2 has a magnitude response of 0.36 (0.22 at 15mM)

The S21 magnitude response of the test cell containing varying concentrations of citric acid-based carbon dots in H2O. There is a clear downward trend in peak response with increasing concentration. However, without knowing the starting concentration of carbon dots, it is difficult to gauge the sensitivity of the method.
The frequency response of 10μm sulfate latex beads suspended in H_2 O. There is a small but visible downward trend with increasing concentration.

Comparison of 10μm and 4μm diameter sulfate latex microparticles. (a) Standard frequency range: There is little distinction between the particle sizes. Inset shows some differentiation between particle sizes. (b) High frequency range: there is more differentiation with 10μm particles showing a larger magnitude response than the 4μm particles.

head.ce:title: High frequency electrochemical transmission spectroscopy application to bulk conducting analytes in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Electrical and Computer Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dept. of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDept. of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDept. of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work illustrates a novel technique, high-frequency transmission spectroscopy (HFTS), for the characterization of analyte in aqueous solution. The application of this technique to material characterization is similar traditional impedance spectroscopy (IS) but differs primarily in that HFTS is a measure of transmitted potential through the material (voltage out versus voltage in) rather than its impedance (current out versus voltage in). The material properties explored by HFTS are not limited to effects occurring at the electrode/analyte interface which dominate IS measurements. Additionally, electric network analyzers (ENA), used for the characterization of frequency response, provide spectrum measurement capability over a much higher frequency range than typical impedance instrumentation. In this work, the capability of HFTS is demonstrated using three test cases: electrolyte solutions, carbon nanoparticles in solution, and sulfate latex microparticles in aqueous suspension. The experimental setup consists of a 3D printed liquid test cell, custom design patch antennae, and an off-the shelf ENA with a frequency range of 300 kHz to 500 MHz. The magnitude response of these analytes at various concentrations demonstrated a sensitivity of the measurement on the order of 10 µM. Furthermore, the system shows the ability to distinguish between monovalent and divalent ions and detect changes in radii on the order of 10 pm to 1 µm. Techniques demonstrated in this work have a wide range of potential applications including environmental monitoring, detection of polluting substances (e.g. PFAS) in water, and disease/infection screening.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: This work presents a novel technique, referred to herein as high-frequency transmission spectroscopy (HFTS), for the characterization of materials in aqueous solution. HFTS involves the application of techniques traditionally applied to the characterization of radio frequency (RF) equipment to material characterization. Practitioners of materials characterization will find HFTS similar to the traditional impedance spectroscopy (IS) techniques pioneered by J. R. Macdonald [1,2] in which an AC potential perturbation of varying frequency is applied to a material and the resulting current is measured. HFTS fundamentally differs from IS in that a potential is measured at a secondary location within the material rather than the current resulting from the applied perturbation. This technique provides several key advantages over traditional IS such as a simplified, 2-port experimental setup, and the capability to obtain measurements of phenomena at higher frequency ranges which are not dominated by electrochemical effects at the electrode-material interface. In this work, the feasibility of HFTS is demonstrated through its application to identification of electrolyte solutions as well as measuring the relative concentrations of carbon nanoparticles and sulfate-latex microparticles in suspension.

Fundamentally, HFTS takes the form of a traditional 2-port electrical network analysis commonly used in the engineering field for the characterization of electronic filters [3]. A block diagram of this type of analysis is shown in Fig. 1 . AC potential perturbation is applied on one of the ports and the resulting potential or current is measured. When a potential is measured given an AC potential perturbation, this measurement is referred to as a scattering-parameter (S-parameter) [4]. When the S-parameter is measured on the same port as the applied AC potential (S11, S22), this measurement represents the reflectance of the network at the perturbation frequency. Likewise, when the S-parameter is measured on the opposite port as the potential (S21, S12), the measurement represents the frequency-dependent transmittance of the network. In this work, the S21 parameter is used to measure the transmittance of the material-of-interest (MOI) although measurement of the reflectance may also yield some valuable insights and will be explored in future work.

There are a wide range of potential applications of HFTS to problems in biochemistry, medicine, materials, and environmental science. In the previous literature, S-parameter measurements using high-frequency perturbations have been shown to be sensitive to the size of cellular nuclei [5], identification of gene expression in cDNA [6], and has been theorized to be able to detect tumors in both the brain [7] and lungs [8]. S-parameters measurements have been successfully utilized to measure the thickness of walls in building structures [9] and identify potential faults due to corrosion in electric cables [10]. The growth stages of corn [11] and wheat [12] crops have been successfully classified via advanced analysis built on top of S-parameter data.

HFTS is similar in many respects to IS and measurements utilizing S-parameters have sometimes been reported as IS measurements [13] (Readers unfamiliar with IS should refer to this details background found in the recent review by Lazanas and Prodromidis [14]). However, HFTS differs from IS in several key respects. Primarily, measurement of currents in response to potential perturbation is not required for the acquisition of S-parameters. The two ports of the S-parameter measurement are kept electrically isolated from each other and the “measured” signal is, in-fact, a secondary potential perturbation caused by the transmission of the AC electromagnetic field through the MOI.

A simplified lumped element circuit model of the HFTS measurement is shown in Fig. 2 . In this model, Vs is the AC potential perturbation, Z0 are the input and output impedance of the measurement instrumentation and Zm is the impedance of the MOI. V1 is the excitation potential experience on port 1 of the MOI and V2 is the excitation potential transmitted through the MOI and received at port 2. From this schematic, a mathematical model of the HFTS measurement can be made.
(1)
S 21
=
V 2
/
V 1
(2)
V 1
=
V s
Z m
+
Z 0
Z m
+ 2
Z 0
(3)
V 2
=
V s
Z 0
Z m
+ 2
Z 0

If the standard input/output impedance for high-frequency instrumentation of 50Ω is assigned to Z0, then the model for the S21 measurement can be reduced to:
(4)
S 21
(
Z m
)
=
50
100 +
Z m
From Eq. 4 we see that the S21 parameter is dependent only on the impedance of the MOI. This demonstrates that the HFTS measurement result is dependent only on the material properties of the MOI.

Additionally, the S21 parameter is frequency dependent which, assuming that the MOI can be treated as a linear system, the HFTS measurement represents the frequency response of the MOI.
(5)
S 21
( f )
= H
( f )

The frequency response is a complex value which can be decomposed into its magnitude and phase response.
(6)
|
H
( f )
|
=
r e a
l
2
{
H
( f )
}
+ i m a
g
2
{
H
( f )
}
(7)
∡ H
( f )
=
tan
− 1
(
i m a g
{
H
( f )
}
r e a l
{
H
( f )
}
)

These components can be plotted separately in the form of a Bode plot or together in polar form as a Nyquist plot [15]. In this work, for demonstrative purposes, only the magnitude response of the HFTS measurement is considered although future work may incorporate the phase information in order to provide a more complete picture of the properties of the MOI

These differences offer several key advantages over traditional IS methods. By electrically coupling the working electrode to the MOI, the measurement is dominated by electrochemical effects occurring at the electrode/material interface which result in additional impedances such as double layer capacitance [16-18] and electron transfer resistance [19,20]. These effects are often a result of redox reactions and therefore, the properties of the electrode must be considered carefully. In HFTS, the material properties of the electrode may be disregarded since it will generally remain static and not alter the measured S-parameters. Additionally, the application of preexisting electrical network instrumentation enables measurements across a much wider range of frequencies from the low kilohertz to tens of gigahertz for higher-end devices. Available Impedance analyzers typically are limited to low megahertz ranges. At these higher frequencies, atomic-scale material properties may be probed [21]. An HFTS measurement is always a two-port measurement and does not require the three or four electrode configurations often required in IS measurements. These additional electrodes add additional complexity to the experimental setup, limits materials for possible analysis, and increases the complexity of the analysis and circuit models [22,23].

The work presented in this paper involves the characterization of material properties of analytes in aqueous solution/suspension. First, repeated measurement of 18MΩ H2O will be used to demonstrate the repeatability of the HFTS experimental setup. Then, measurements of varying concentrations of electrolytes in solution will demonstrate that the HFTS measurement is sensitive to changes in concentration of the same analyte in solution as well as molecular differences in the analyte such as charge and atomic radius. It is extremely difficult to determine the concentration of carbon nanoparticles in liquid suspension. In this work, it will be demonstrated that HFTS measurement is sensitive to changes in concentration of these particles. Finally, measurement sensitivity to concentration sulfate-latex microparticles in suspension will be demonstrated to show the feasibility of HFTS measurement to environmental contaminants such as polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS).

The experimental setup for this study consists of a custom-designed and manufactured test cell connected via two, 3ft long coaxial cables terminated on both ends with Subminiature version A (SMA) connectors to an ENA (E5063A, Keysight Technologies). A system-level block diagram of the setup is given in Fig. 3 . The E5063A provides an s-parameter measurement capability over a frequency range of 50 kHz to 500 MHz at a default signal strength of -5 dBm. At the standard input impedance of 50 Ω, this results in a peak excitation potential of 356 mV. Note that, unlike in traditional IS measurements, no DC potential is applied to the test cell.

The test cell comprises a volume to hold the liquid sample and a space to insert the two electrode antennae. The volume is a 2 mL rectangular cavity of dimensions 1 cm by 1 cm by 2 cm. The test cell was 3D printed (Form 3+, FormLabs) using SLA resin. After printing, the test cell was washed for 15 minutes in isopropyl alcohol (FormWash, FormLabs), and cured for 30 minutes at 60 degrees Celsius under 375 nm UV irradiation (FormCure system, FormLabs) (Fig. 9).

The electrodes are custom-designed PCBs modeled after patch-style antennas terminated with SMA connectors. The patch formed from a 1 cm by 1 cm copper trace on an FR4 dielectric substrate with a backing ground plane. Each electrode is inserted into the volume such that it forms one of the 1 cm2 sides of the test cell cavity and is in direct contact with the aqueous solution. A thin layer of silkscreen covers the copper trace and forms an additional dielectric layer between the electrode and the sample. This helps to reduce any contact-impedance effects or unwanted electrochemical reactions on the response of the test cell.

In the case of the test setup presented in this work, the series impedance of the electrode antennae and test cell will be taken together as an equivalent impedance. However, the impedance of the antennae, while varying across the range of frequencies, should remain consistent across measurements and any change in measured impedance at specific frequencies should result solely in from changes to the test cell impedance.

To run an experiment, the test cell is cleaned before use via rinsing in ultra-pure water (device, brand) and wiped down for a minimum of 10 s using a sterile paper towel. The liquid sample volume is added by pipette to ensure exactly 2 mL of sample are added to the test cell sample volume in each test. For all tests reported in this study, the ENA's excitation potential sweep was set to the default power level of -5 dBm with 2000 samples taken over a linear frequency range of 50 kHz to 500 MHz. All measurements were recorded as complex s-parameter values and stored in separate .CSV data files. Data processing was performed in Python 3.12 using the NumPy and Pandas libraries.

NaCl, MgCl2, and NaNO3 salts were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Solutions were prepared in ultrapure 18MOhm water. Citric acid, formamide, and acetone were purchased from Thermo-Fisher. All salt solutions were prepared using ultrapure water at concentrations of 150 mM/L. From this starting concentration, 10-fold dilutions were performed to obtain lower concentrations.

Carbon dot nanoparticles of 2-3 nm size were synthesized by dissolving citric acid into formamide, followed by microwave irradiation to form nanoparticles. Carbon dots were precipitated using acetone, and centrifuged before resuspending in ultrapure water using previously described methods [24]. Carbon dots derived from citric acid precursors are known to have a negative charge that prevents aggregation. The net negative surface charge maintains particle suspension.

10-micron sulfate modified latex microspheres (LOT S37230, Life Technologies) were used as provided by the manufacturer. Using Eq. 4 where W is the concentration of particles, p is the polymer density of the sulfate latex (1.055 g/mL), and D is the mean diameter of the particles, the number of particles per mL is found to be 7.24 10 3 P / mL [25].
(8)
N p
=
W v
% · 6 ×
10
6
π
ρ p
D
The starting solution was diluted with ultrapure water to obtain solutions of 60 % and 20 % concentration by number.

To characterize the test cell, a precision/repeatability analysis was performed using a test cell containing only 2 mL of ultrapure water. Ten measurements were taken with the entire experimental setup being dismantled and reassembled in-between each measurement with at least one electrode antenna that was not used in the previous measurement. After 5 measurements, the SMA-terminated coaxial cables were moved to the opposite ENA port. This was to maximize the noise introduced both by the experimenter and different pieces of test equipment. To ensure measurement reliability, the ENA was warmed up for 90 minutes prior to data collection and was kept running during the entire experiment. At each frequency point, the signal-to-noise ratio is calculated for the magnitude response using Eq. 5 where µ is the mean response and σ is the standard deviation of the response. This work will consider the industry standard 40dB as the threshold for an acceptable SNR.
(9)
S N R = 20 · log
(
μ σ
)

For 10 repeated measurements of ultrapure water in the test cell, the mean SNR was found to be 58.5 dB (sigma = 18.8 dB). The peak SNR of 105.3 dB occurred at approximately 60 MHz. This frequency also showed the peak magnitude response of 0.86. The minimum SNR of 5.1 dB occurred at approximately 100 MHz. Fig. 4 shows the mean magnitude response and mean SNR of the test cell. Outside of a few frequency segments, the experimental setup shows an acceptable SNR greater than 40 dB. The peak magnitude/SNR point suggests that this is the resonant frequency of the system which would correspond to the probable maximum sensitivity band of the system. While data has been collected over the entire frequency range of 50 kHz to 500 MHz, only measurements within a contiguous band containing the resonant frequency of 60 MHz which also showed an SNR > 40 dB will be considered in the analysis given in this section. This frequency range is found to be 1.3 MHz to 94.5 MHz, spaced linearly over a frequency step size of 250 kHz (372 samples per measurement).

Analysis of the antennas reveals a natural resonance beginning at approximately 7 GHz with harmonic resonances at higher frequencies. If the 2 mL test cell is treated as a cavity waveguide, it has a resonant frequency of approximately 2.5 GHz. These resonant frequencies are well outside of the 500 MHz bandwidth of the ENA utilized in this work. However, there are two resonance peaks clearly visible in Fig. 4 at approximately 60 MHz and 250 MHz. These anomalous resonance frequencies are likely due to shunt capacitances present in the interface between the test cell and the ENA.

The results of a simulation performed in LTSpice (Linear Technologies, 2024) to explore the effect of shunt capacitance on resonance frequency. In this simulation, the complex impedances of the antennae, ZA1 and ZA2, and test cell cavity are approximated as LC bandpass filters with resonant frequencies at 5 GHz and 3 GHz. At the input and output ports, shunt capacitors with matching values of 1 fF were applied and then increased in 1 pF steps to 50 pF. Fig. 5 shows the magnitude response for shunt capacitance values of 1 pF and 50 pF. In this figure, the decrease in resonance frequency with increase in shunt capacitance is clearly visible.

Simulation results showed a decrease in resonant frequency with increasing shunt capacitance at a rate of 45 MHz/pF. This suggests that a decrease in resonance frequency from 7 GHz to approximately 250 MHz would require an increase in shunt capacitance of 150 pF. From Fig. 5, it can be also observed that the frequency space between resonant peaks decreases with increasing capacitance. From this, it is reasonable to expect that the observed two resonance peaks in the HFTS measurement with a frequency spacing of approximately 200 MHz.

There is significant parasitic capacitance in the 3ft coaxial cables used to couple the ENA to the test cell antennae. This capacitance was measured under DC conditions and found to be 120nF. Ordinarily, this would be considered a shortcoming of an experimental high-frequency setup. However, it has been shown that measurements of analyte concentration are most sensitive at the resonant frequency of the instrumentation [26]. In this case, the parasitic capacitance provides an shifting of the resonant frequency so that it is observable inside the 500 MHz range. This shifting is advantageous to the HFTS measurement.

To test the sensitivity of this method to changes in concentration of bulk electrolyte, an aqueous solution of NaCl at varying concentrations was examined in Fig. 6 . At concentrations below
150
m M
, there is a magnitude peak centered at approximately
60
M H z
, consistent with the SNR analysis. A decrease in magnitude response and resonant frequency is observed with increase in concentration with a particularly dramatic change from 15mM to 150mM concentrations.

A least-squares linear regression performed on the data in Table 1 shows a strong inverse correlation between changes in concentration and changes in magnitude response. If a linear concentration is done between the concentration and the peak value of the magnitude response for data in Table 1, a slope of
0.0249 ± 0.005
M
− 1
is found with an intercept of
0.0204 ± 0.004
M
and an R2 value of
0.88
. This suggests that at concentrations below approximately
20
m M
the system provides a linear response to changes in salt concentration. Furthermore, the device is sensitive to changes in concentration. The calculated slope and the sensitivity of the ENA suggest an ability to distinguish concentration changes of salts on the order of
250
μ M
.

Also shown is a considerable change in the frequency of peak magnitude response and an apparent reversal of the concentration/response relationship when the concentration increases to 150 mM. The peak magnitude response of this concentration is 0.46 at a frequency of 14.5 MHz. This is consistent with the linear regression analysis as well, which suggests that the HFTS has a change of behavior for concentrations above 20 mM. It should be noted that in this range, it appears that the HFTS is capable of measuring concentrations, but the linear relationship is no longer maintained. Interestingly, at 150 mM there is a shift in response that was observed in high-frequency impedance work by [24].

Various properties of molecules in solution such as size and charge may contribute to the complex impedance and impact the frequency response of the test cell. To explore this, several electrolyte solutions were prepared at concentrations of 150 mM and 15 mM. The relevant molecular properties are summarized in Table 2 . Four direct comparisons are made. NaCl and KCl are compared to explore the effect of cation size. NaCL and NaNO3, which have similar charge and size in solution, compare anion molarity, since there will be twice as much nitride as chloride in the solution. NaCl and MgCl2 compare cation charge. MgCl2 and Mg2(NO3)2 compare electrolytes with similar molecular properties.

Fig. 7 shows the results of all comparative measurements. Several general observations can be made across all tests. The lower concentration of 15 mM yielded minimal distinction between the analyte solutions, despite being well within the threshold for which concentration changes were observed previously. However, at the higher concentration of 150 mM, there are clear and consistent behaviors that can be distinguished. Again, there is a concentration-dependent frequency shift away from the resonance value at the 150 mM concentrations, similar to that observed by Saghafi et al [24]. Therefore, at least for the electro-physical-chemical changes attempting to be discriminated in these measurements, HFTS could only observe them at higher concentrations. It is possible however that the change in resonant peak made the HFTS more sensitive to a timescale with these mechanisms, rather than higher concentration.

For NaCl and KCL solutions of a given concentration (Fig. 7a), decreasing cation size while maintain all other parameters resulted in larger signal magnitudes for the NaCl at both concentrations. However, when a +2 cation (Mg2+) is compared to Na+ (Fig. 7c), the smaller cation produces a smaller signal near 20 MHz. This suggests that charge plays a larger role in modulating the solution response, which is observed in other methods such as electrophoresis.

Anion behavior did not mirror that of cations. As shown in Fig. 6b, the signal increases with increasing anion radius. However, the radii of nitrate and chloride are within 1 % of each other, therefore the large signal change does not seem reasonable to be ascribed to differences in ionic radii. It is possible that the effective charge of a polyatomic anion differs from the chloride ion. The effect is mitigated when MgNO3 salts are used, where the effective concentration of nitrate and chloride ions is 300 mM.

The result of the carbon dot nanoparticle analysis is shown in Fig. 8
and summarized in Table 3 . Similar to electrolyte concentration analysis, there is a visible inverse correlation between concentration and magnitude response at a frequency of approximately 60 MHz. Examining the inset a change in resonant frequency can be observed, along with the attenuation of the magnitude response. Linear regression analysis on this data showed an R2 value of 0.99 with a slope of
− 6.9 ± 0.4
%
− 1
and an intercept of
5.8 ± 0.3
%
. This shows that a strong linear relationship exists between nanoparticle concentration and magnitude response with an expected sensitivity to concentration changes of approximately 7 %. These results suggest that HFTS can be used to determine the concentration of carbon dot nanoparticles in aqueous solution with a relatively high sensitivity similar to the electrolyte results.

Fig. 8 depicts the magnitude response of the test cell to changing concentrations of microparticles. There is an inverse correlation between particle concentrations and magnitude response. This relationship is summarized in Table 4 . Linear regression analysis showed an R2 value of 1.0 with a slope of
− 2.9 ×
10
5
± 0
N
p
− 1
and an intercept of
2.5 ×
10
5
± 0
N p
. These results suggest that there is a strong linear relationship between concentration of microparticles and the magnitude response of the test cell.

A potential application of HFTS is the characterization of bulk living cells. By passing the high-frequency signals through the cells and measuring the frequency response, it may be possible to determine metrics such as type, size, and health. However, the variability of cell samples as well as their viability in the current test cell make it difficult to obtain a gauge of the test cell's suitability for the application at this stage of development. As an analog for living cells, sulfate latex microparticles with a diameter of 10 um were used.

An analysis of the magnitude response of the test cell is depicted in Fig. 10 a. There is a small but measurable difference between the frequency responses of the 10 um and 4 um particles. The larger 10 um particles give a lower frequency response. This is consistent with the previous analysis of electrolytes at higher concentrations where the larger molecule also yielded a lower response. However, exploratory analysis of the entire frequency measurement is depicted in Fig. 10b. There is a secondary peak in magnitude response around 260 MHz where a larger discrimination between particle size is visible. In contrast to lower frequency results, the larger 10 um particles show a higher peak magnitude response than the smaller 4 um particles. While analysis of the magnitude response of the test cell to microspheres shows a significantly lower sensitivity as compared to electrolyte solutions or carbon dot nanoparticles, there are still concentration effects, and the test cell is able to discern between particles of different sizes. It may be that changes to test cell geometry may increase sensitivity in future work. This hypothesis may be explored in future work.

This work demonstrated the feasibility of HFTS measurements to bulk analyte in aqueous solutions. The use of transmitted potential vs traditional impedance measurement enables observation of effects which are not limited by electrochemical processes at the electrode/analyte interface. Furthermore, due to the use of measurement around the system resonant frequency, HFTS is extremely sensitive to analyte properties. This included distinguishing concentrations to 10µM, the difference between monovalent and divalent ions, and radii down to pm for electrolytes and several microns for micro-particles. Importantly, the strong linear relationship between the magnitude response at the system resonance and concentration for all tested systems indicates that this technique is both robust to system composition and easy to calibrate with new materials. Such features make it highly attractive for a wide range of future materials.

Given these initial results and properties, using HFTS as a means of identifying unknown particles, characterizing particle size/charge, or monitoring solutions for specific components within complicated system are highly exciting potential use cases with applications in blood borne illness diagnosis, water pollution monitoring, and nanoparticle characterization. To enable such functionality, future work will build upon these demonstrations by exploring means to increase the ability to identify particles and improve system sensitivity. As has been used in IS, classification and regression via machine learning analysis will be explored to enable these features in the future. The role of external capacitance as a means of tuning the resonant frequency of the test cell and its potential for increasing the sensitivity of the measurement will be explored.

Derek Johnston: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Tanja Karp: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Dimitri Pappas: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Gordon Christopher: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Gordon Christopher has patent pending to Texas Tech University. Dimitris Pappas has patent pending to Texas Tech University. Derek Johnson has patent pending to Texas Tech University. There is a provisional patent application for the 3 authors listed based on this technology. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. R. Macdonald, "Impedance Spectroscopy," Ann. Biomed. Eng., vol. 20, no. 3, pp. 289-305, 1992, https://doi.org/10.1007/BF02368532.
